item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a minor issue:

Former members of Huǒjù zhi Zi

If you are using pinyin, this should probably be Huǒjù Zhī Zǐ or Huoju Zhi Zi. Also, "Zhurong" for god of fire 祝融, and Zhulong is the dragon 烛龙..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took me some time to realise that you are probably talking about 祝融. The pinyin of it will be Zhu Rong, not Zhulong (which kinda sounds like pig sty to be honest).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair play - you can hardly be blamed for not writing exactly what I wanted to read. The only reason I raised it is that it seemed like those were the themes you were going for, and I thought a change of focus might better suit those themes.

As for the themes you mention above, I didn't get that strongly enough from the article. It's very difficult to create a character that initially seems godlike and then is shown to be human in such a short length. At the moment Zhulong comes across as quite human throughout - there isn't enough change between the two conversations. And we never see David and Emily's reaction to finding out that their deity is fallible - that would be really interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That may be the story you preferred, but it's not the one I was writing. For all of SCP-3965's godlike power, he makes an extremely human miscalculation, all stemming from simple rage. As speechless as David and Emily are in SCP-3965's presence, the humans and the dragon are not different from each other. The followers long to see Zhulong because he is a beacon of hope to them, and Zhulong wants to see the followers because they remind him of a time long past where he brought joy. Zhulong acts out of sorrow for his own past, indignation towards SCP-1815 and love for David and Emily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Roget that the two supplements seem to belong in the article - they are necessary to form a complete story and need to be read in a particular order.

I downvoted, for the reasons I outlined on the forums. The characters are fairly one-dimensional, particularly -1, which is yet another god-like alien intelligence that prefers to communicate in modern English full sentences, with a preference for metaphor and an avuncular tone. And the giant space-monster battle failed to elicit any emotional response from me. As I said, I would have preferred a story about David and Emily exploring emotions and themes relating to legacy, tradition and passing the torch, with -1 as a non-speaking catalyst.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's definitely more here than before and I can appreciate it as a framing device but I also can't help but feel the article would be better had the supplements been folded into the main article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: man i really like this series of stuff, i love this big friendly sun dragon +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. This is true, however, I feel that the way SCP-1815 is briefly described here (a parasitic, cancerous thing that is feeding off the sun and Mercury) gives enough context so that the reader doesn't really need to read 1815 to enjoy this, and if they do read 1815, it's relatively short.

2. This is also true. However, I left some contextual clues that Dr. Albert isn't exactly a stranger to subverting the Foundation's general position on some things. He stops the security people from intervening during the song portion, and flat out breaks outpost protocol to reach the interview site in Baoying. The only reason he isn't punished for that is A) the outcome of the event, and B) the fact they would have all died from radiation poisoning if they followed outpost protocol. He is the Foundation's leading expert on these artifacts, which is why they let him get away with stuff from time to time. He also has a genuine respect for their ideology, which is why he genuflects to SCP-3965, and why he permitted such a personal interview in the first place. Also, I should note that it was their fifth interview, so they would have gotten all the necessary stuff out of the way already, and could have an interview devoted purely to research (including cultural research).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two complaints:

1) This feels too reliant on 1815.

2) I'm not quite buying that the Foundation would allow personnel from an anomalous religious sect to interact with an anomaly tied to that sect, and certainly not in as personal a manner as depicted in the supplements. They should be Foundation first, if they're trusted enough to be considered personnel.

Execution is otherwise good, but I can't get over these two things.

item.title: Re: Minor Critique
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-3965 is largely dormant, remaining motionless for >95% of documented observation."

Perhaps you can elaborate on why it seems vague. It seems rather comprehensible, that it is motionless in over 95% of observations documented by the Foundation.

Do you want something like the actual number of observations or something?

item.title: Re: Minor Critique
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I can help you with that one in particular.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Upon further inspection, the copyright status seems to be murky at best; image replaced with something suitable.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Aha, so it does, on the regular site- but not the mobile one (which I was using). Well, that's sorted then!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: It mentions on the page that the images are public domain.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Ah, unfortunately, that source doesn't seem to be complient- the copyright holder hasn't mentioned a license anywhere.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Source catalogued.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

EDIT: Images all okay

item.title: Minor Critique
item.content:encoded:
I largely enjoyed this SCP, especially the attached Log, but something rubs me wrong about "SCP-3965 is largely dormant, remaining motionless for >95% of documented observation."

It doesn't seem very realistic for them to have gathered enough data to get that percentage and then define it in such a vague way, especially when they are only referring to their own documentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know little about the Children of the Torch and Xia, but it was still enjoyable. The imagery throughout, especially at the end, was also very striking, and the worldbuilding was well done.

+1

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
First Article Image

Original Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mercury_in_color_-_Prockter07-edit1.jpg
Creator: NASA

Creator: NASA

Original Source: https://pixabay.com/en/lightning-zipper-thunderstorm-night-2555826/
Creator: Shkiper

Supplement 2 Image

Original Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Braer_Storm_1993-01-11.gif
Creator: meteosat-4 satellite

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanking SunnyClockwork, FlameShirt, mlister, DrBleep, Modern_Erasmus, Hippo, Roget and everyone else that helped me with this one. I'll have the image attributions up in just a minute.

Children of the Torch Series

SCP-1428 Jinwu
SCP-2814 Heretic of the Torch
SCP-2995 Radiant Son
SCP-3418 Devourer of Flames

Xia ACG Series (by SunnyClockwork)

SCP-2481 Kill the Suns
SCP-2711 定海神针 如意金箍棒
SCP-2841 Revenge of the Snakes
SCP-2847 Yu the Great, and the Immortal
SCP-3847 If only I could talk with birds

Here's some mood music for the article! So Majestic (spoilers for FFXIV Stormblood)
